sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Falls risk assessment in FIFOEMS should identify patients whose balance, strength, cognition, medications, environment, or acute illness make them unsafe to remain unsupported.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Recent falls, near-falls, or fear of falling
- Reduced balance, gait speed, or transfer ability
- Use of walking aid or furniture walking
- Dizziness, syncope history, polypharmacy, or lower-limb weakness

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Falls are often multifactorial and may reflect medical illness as much as poor balance
- A physiotherapy falls assessment should still screen for delirium, infection, dehydration, cardiac causes, and orthostatic issues
- One fall may be the first sign of a much bigger decline in function or safety

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Ask about recent falls, injury, circumstances, walking aids, and confidence with transfers
- Screen vitals, acute illness clues, and immediate mobility safety
- Escalate if the patient cannot transfer safely, has new neurological symptoms, or the fall may reflect medical instability

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Use structured tools such as TUG, Berg, Tinetti, SPPB, or frailty scoring where relevant to follow-up and planning
- Identify modifiable drivers such as footwear, vision, medication timing, environmental hazards, weakness, and orthostatic symptoms
- Develop a falls-prevention and supervision plan and liaise for medical review when risk remains high

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Falls assessment should end with a practical safety plan, not just a score
